skill.name: web-accessibility
skill.description: Guides accessible web UI implementation using WCAG 2.1, semantic HTML, ARIA, keyboard navigation, screen reader support, and accessibility testing practices.
skill.summary: Build and audit accessible web interfaces with WCAG, ARIA, keyboard, and screen reader guidance.
skill.author: supercent-io

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, filesystem, network, sensitive-data, and weak-crypto patterns. Manual review found these patterns inside Markdown examples, accessibility terms, reference links, and relative documentation links, with no executable scripts or malicious intent found. The skill is low risk but should remain transparent about external documentation links and illustrative code snippets.

low_findings.title: Static command and crypto alerts are documentation false positives
low_findings.description: The reported command-execution and weak-cryptography alerts occur in Markdown fences, inline HTML tags, ARIA names, and example code. No skill file contains an executable script, shell command, cryptographic operation, or runtime command dispatcher.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged lines are visible documentation or sample UI code, not executable skill logic. The repository contains only SKILL.md and SKILL.toon, so there is no supporting runtime path for command execution.

low_findings.title: External URLs are accessibility reference links
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs point to WCAG, MDN, WebAIM, axe DevTools, and A11y Project resources. They are Markdown references and do not create network requests or data exfiltration behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines are plain Markdown links in a References section. No evidence found of automatic fetch, upload, telemetry, or credential transmission.

low_findings.title: Relative paths are related-skill documentation links
low_findings.description: The path traversal alerts are relative Markdown links to neighboring skill documents. They are not file-read operations, writes, imports, or instructions to access sensitive paths.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The ../ sequences are confined to a Related Skills section. No evidence found of filesystem APIs, shell commands, or user-controlled path construction.

low_findings.title: Keyboard and ARIA terms triggered sensitive-data and reconnaissance rules
low_findings.description: Terms such as e.key, aria-describedby, tabindex, outline, and keyboard testing match scanner patterns. In context they describe accessibility behavior, not credential access, system inspection, or network reconnaissance.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged words appear in accessibility guidance and front-end test examples. No evidence found of secrets, certificate files, host probing, or active network scanning.

content.value_statement: Many web interfaces exclude users who rely on keyboards, screen readers, or high contrast settings. This skill guides WCAG-focused implementation, review, and testing for accessible UI components.
actual_capabilities: Explains when to use semantic HTML instead of generic div and span elements.
Provides keyboard navigation patterns for dropdowns, modals, tabs, and forms.
Shows ARIA labeling, descriptions, live regions, hidden content, and dialog attributes.
Lists WCAG contrast targets and visual accessibility requirements.
Suggests automated and manual accessibility tests using axe, Lighthouse, keyboard checks, and screen readers.
Produces accessibility checklists and implementation guidance for common web components.
limitations: It does not run automated accessibility scanners by itself.
It does not guarantee legal compliance without human review.
It focuses on WCAG 2.1 guidance, not every regional accessibility law.
Screen reader behavior still needs validation on real devices and browsers.

use_cases.title: Create accessible UI components
use_cases.description: Generate accessible patterns for forms, modals, dropdowns, tabs, and navigation before implementation starts.

use_cases.title: Review WCAG gaps before release
use_cases.description: Convert an accessibility concern into a checklist covering semantics, keyboard access, ARIA, contrast, and testing.

use_cases.title: Define acceptance criteria
use_cases.description: Turn product requirements into accessibility rules that engineering teams can implement and test.

prompt_templates.title: Check a simple component
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner review of one UI element.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this button or link for WCAG AA accessibility. Include semantic HTML, label, focus, and keyboard requirements.

prompt_templates.title: Improve a form
prompt_templates.prompt: Make this form accessible for screen readers and keyboard users. Include labels, errors, hints, required fields, and validation messages.

prompt_templates.title: Design a modal pattern
prompt_templates.prompt: Design an accessible modal for my framework. Include focus trap behavior, ESC handling, ARIA attributes, and testing steps.

prompt_templates.title: Audit an interface
prompt_templates.prompt: Audit this page against WCAG 2.1 AA. Return prioritized issues, user impact, fixes, and manual and automated test coverage.

output_examples.input: A product form needs accessibility review before launch.
output: Checks whether each field has a connected visible label.
Recommends descriptive error messages announced with live regions.
Adds keyboard and screen reader validation steps.

output_examples.input: A React modal closes with a mouse but has no keyboard behavior.
output: Requires focus to move into the modal when it opens.
Adds Escape close behavior and focus return after close.
Defines dialog labels and a keyboard-only test checklist.

output_examples.input: A tab interface needs WCAG AA support.
output: Defines tab, tablist, and tabpanel roles.
Adds arrow key, Home, and End navigation expectations.
Includes tests for focus order and selected state announcements.

best_practices: Start with native semantic HTML before adding ARIA attributes.
Test every interactive workflow with keyboard-only navigation.
Combine automated scanner results with manual screen reader checks.
anti_patterns: Do not remove focus outlines without providing a visible replacement.
Do not use placeholder text as the only form label.
Do not rely on color alone to communicate errors or status.

faq.question: What accessibility standard does this skill target?
faq.answer: It focuses on WCAG 2.1 guidance, especially common AA implementation needs for web interfaces.

faq.question: Can it create accessible React components?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes React-style examples for dropdowns, modals, forms, notifications, and tabs.

faq.question: Does it replace axe, Lighthouse, or screen reader testing?
faq.answer: No. It helps plan and fix issues, but real automated and manual testing is still required.

faq.question: Does it cover keyboard accessibility?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers Tab, Shift+Tab, Enter, Space, arrow keys, Escape, focus order, and focus management.

faq.question: Does it explain ARIA attributes?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers labels, descriptions, live regions, hidden content, dialogs, tabs, and selected states.

faq.question: Can teams use it for compliance work?
faq.answer: It can support compliance preparation, but legal compliance requires expert review and organization-specific validation.
